coredata.dc:title: Development of nitrogen doped carbon dots modified CuCo alloy nanoparticles for potential electrocatalytic water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Naqvi, Sayed Tayyab Raza
Rasheed, Tahir
Majeed, Saadat
Rani, Zubia
Nawaz, Rahat
Ashiq, Muhammad Naeem
coredata.dc:description: Water splitting in recent years has been emerged as a promising approach for renewable energy sources. A number of materials have been synthesized for successful electrosplitting of water. Yet, the simultaneous production of hydrogen and oxygen with the help of efficient and stable bi-functional electrocatalyst is still challenging. Correspondingly, the precious metal based catalysts are gaining much interest for the efficient inexpensive and ecofriendlier practical application in energy materials among researchers. Herein, we report new combination of nitrogen doped carbon dots modified copper cobalt (NDCDs-CuCo) alloy nanoparticles as potential electrocatalyst for water splitting. These catalysts were synthesized at different temperatures. The NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles exhibit excellent catalytic performances and stability in alkaline medium for hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions (HER and OER) through synergistic effect between nitrogen doped carbon dots and bimetallic CuCo alloy nanoparticles at optimized conditions of onset over potentials and current densities. NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles possess electrocatalytic properties in strong alkaline medium with current density of 10 mA cm−2 at 0.38 V.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water splitting
HER
OER
Over-potential
Electrochemical water splitting
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Copper cobalt alloy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs)
Synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (NDCDs)-CuCo alloy nanoparticles
Synthesis and morphological characterization of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles
Electrochemical activity of NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles for water splitting

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Office of Research Innovation and Commercialization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was financially supported by Office of Research Innovation and Commercialization (ORIC), Bahauddin Zakariya University Multan, Pakistan under grant No. DR & EL/D-740 and Institute of Chemical Sciences, Bahauddin Zakariya University Multan, Pakistan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) NDCDs and (b) CuCo alloy nanoparticles and NDCD-CuCo alloy at (c) 300 °C (d) 400 °C and (e) 500 °C.
Linear calibration curve for effect of pH at (a) 7.4, (b) 7.8 and (c) 8 in basic media of 0.1 M PBS NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticle potential window −1 V to +1 V, scan rate 0.1 mV/s.

Electrochemical response at varied loading amount of 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 μL of catalyst. CV curves for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles modified electrode (a), of linear calibration graph (b): RHE response for catalyst (c) keeping all other conditions constant: at 300 °C, potential window −1 V to +1 V, scan rate 0.1 mV/s.

Electrochemical response (a), (b), (c) and linear calibration curve (a′),(b′),(c′) of NDCDs -CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C respectively, at different scan rates of (0.06, 0.07, 0.08, 0.09 and 0.1 mV/s) in 1 M KOH (pH = 13.65) and potential window −1 V to +1 V.

Linear sweep voltammetry curves of NDCDs modified with CuCo at temperatures (a) 300 °C, (b) 400 °C (c) 500 °C and corresponding Tafel slops of NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at (a′) 300 °C (b′) 400 °C (c′) 500 °C at different scan rates of 0.06, 0.07, 0.08, 0.09, 0.1 mV/s in alkaline solution of 1 M KOH (pH = 13.65) and potential window of −1 V to +1 V. In all cases the sample loading on the GCE is 0.2 μL.

lectrochemical response of NDCDs, CuCo, and NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at different temperatures 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C by using (a) Ag/AgCl reference electrode and (b) RHE. Conditions: scan rates of 0.1 mV/s in 1 M KOH (pH = 13.65), potential window −1 V to +1 V, sample loading on GCE 0.2 μL.

head.ce:title: Development of nitrogen doped carbon dots modified CuCo alloy nanoparticles for potential electrocatalytic water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Analytical Chemistry, Institute of Chemical Sciences, Bahauddin Zakariya University, Multan 60800, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Analytical Chemistry, Institute of Chemical Sciences, Bahauddin Zakariya University, Multan 60800, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water splitting in recent years has been emerged as a promising approach for renewable energy sources. A number of materials have been synthesized for successful electrosplitting of water. Yet, the simultaneous production of hydrogen and oxygen with the help of efficient and stable bi-functional electrocatalyst is still challenging. Correspondingly, the precious metal based catalysts are gaining much interest for the efficient inexpensive and ecofriendlier practical application in energy materials among researchers. Herein, we report new combination of nitrogen doped carbon dots modified copper cobalt (NDCDs-CuCo) alloy nanoparticles as potential electrocatalyst for water splitting. These catalysts were synthesized at different temperatures. The NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles exhibit excellent catalytic performances and stability in alkaline medium for hydrogen and oxygen evolution reactions (HER and OER) through synergistic effect between nitrogen doped carbon dots and bimetallic CuCo alloy nanoparticles at optimized conditions of onset over potentials and current densities. NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles possess electrocatalytic properties in strong alkaline medium with current density of 10 mA cm−2 at 0.38 V.

ce:section.ce:para: Greener environment and energy are the basic needs of humanity now a days. As a result of advancement in industrialization and human population the energy demand has been increasing day by day. This increase is causing depletion of fossil fuel (petroleum, natural gas and coal) reservoir which in turn causes environmental pollution at high level [1–3]. So, generation of energy from renewable source is burning issue [4]. However, our unsustainable dependence on these resources of energy, creates alarming security, economic and environmental concerns. Fast decline in fossil fuel reservoir is compelling the scientist to seek alternate green and utmost source of energy in the favor of humanity. We can get energy from sunlight to meet the demand of energy as it is green, cost effective and unlimited source but its unavailability at night and cloudy weather make it less profitable [5]. As a result, the movement from solar energy to chemical energy of hydrogen molecule through water splitting has attained remarkable position in energy harvesting as water occupies 72% of earth surface. So, to cope with the scenario, energy from water splitting has become cost effective, green, renewable and endless resources for the purpose [6].

Now days, H2 is recognized as potential source of energy because of its high energy density (141MJ/Kg), pollution free and easy storage as fuel. Traditionally H2 is produced from natural gas and petroleum by using steam reforming method, but it is not suitable for longer time [7,8]. In fact, only 4% of hydrogen is produced by water splitting but mostly it is obtained from fossil fuel [9].Splitting of water can be performed via photocatalysis and electrocatalysis. In photocatalysis utilizes solar energy or light to generate H2 by water splitting. While in electrocatalysis, catalyst consumes electrical energy to split the water. Although, need of high over potential for water splitting during process resulting enormous energy wastage make it less favorable [10]. Furthermore, prevailed catalysts for electrocatalytic water splitting are Nobel metals, offers enhanced catalytic activity, generally low over potential and feasible catalytic kinetics but their preciousness and imperfect cycle stability make them uneconomical [11]. But some noble metal based electrocatalysts depict excellent catalytic attribute toward single half reaction during complete water splitting, such as, Ir and Ru are good for OER as Pt for HER. Single noble metal based electrocatalyst that can lead to catalysis of both HER and OER is rarely reported till now. So, synthesis of electrocatalyst for OER and HER which offers cost effectiveness, good catalytical activity, kinetics and cycle stability is needed to make water catalysis applicable [12].

Apart from all metals, Pt is present at the highest point of the volcano and shows almost zero hydrogen adsorption energy and while on the both sides of the noble metals all other metals can catalyze the water electrolysis with variable hydrogen binding capacity such as the left side of the Pt there are W, Mo, Co, Ni etc. which strongly bind hydrogen while on the right side of the Pt there are Au, Cu, Ag etc. which weakly bind hydrogen. But these noble metal catalysts are not only expensive but also limited. Therefore, the scientific community has started to move toward non-noble metals. Currently, noble free transition metal based nitride, carbide, phosphide and sulphide have been synthesized to possess splendid HER catalysis like MoCx/C, Ni3N, CoP etc. [13–19]. Furthermore, transition metal oxide, bimetallic oxide and hydroxide such as CuO, MnO2, CoFe2O4, ZnCo2O4, NiFe-LDH, NiTi-LDH, CoFe-LDH etc. [20–22], have also been offered super OER catalytic behavior in both acidic and basic environments. Due to electronic band and catalytic properties metal carbides are similar to Pt group metals, show maximum hydrogen-adsorption and electrical conductivity. Mo2C and WC are predicted as non-Pt electrocatalysts for HER [23]. On the other hands, hydroxide and oxide of Ni, Co, Cu and Mn have gained the attention of researcher for OER.

Currently, for the purpose of electrochemical catalytic effect for HER and OER in acidic and alkaline media respectively has gained a lot of interest, by using Co/Ni-WSx,MoC, MoS2, FeS, Ni2P and CoP for HER and nickel, cobalt, manganese, copper, and hydroxides/oxides of cobalt etc. for OER [24]. There are also such catalysts which are capable of both reactions i.e. HER and OER in same media. But the number of such catalysts is too few.

Water splitting depends on charge separation and charge transfer. So, in order to get high charge separation and transfer, variety of nanomaterials and hybrid composites have been applied for water splitting. These materials include carbon based semiconducting materials like nanotubes, nanosheets, nanodots, nanobelts, graphene and nanowires [25]. Fabrication of these carbon based materials with metallic catalyst make the modified electrocatalyst excellent charge separator and causes high charge transfer [26]. Usually, metal based nanoparticles and alloys lose their catalytic behavior due to cluster formation and oxidation during synthetic procedure, long storage and high temperature calcinations. So, in order to overcome issue carbon based conductive support is provided to metallic catalyst for even distribution and to avoid cluster formation during operation [25]. Carbon based materials are easy to modify, cost effective and high stability and being used at wider scale. Doping of support or a base material is another way to enhance active sites which in turn make them satisfactory conductive resulting high charge transfer by electrocatalyst. Mostly, nitrogen (N), sulphur (S) and phosphorus (P) have been executed for doping the materials. Current research revealed that N doping regulates the framework of carbon base material, providing good conductivity to carbon materials. It has proved that N doped carbon material inherited negative charge on adjacent carbon atom to nitrogen which in turn favors the HER [27]. High performance electrocatalysts for complete water splitting can be designed by executing the above described methodology.

Usually, the catalyst which shows good catalytic activity in alkaline media tends to be inactive or unstable in acidic media and vice versa. If we assimilate two such catalysts which are fit for OER and HER respectively, then it results in low catalytic activity [28]. So, the synthesis of bi-functional catalyst which work well in both acidic and basic media remains as a challenge. To meet the demand, such catalysts should be synthesized which can effectively split water into hydrogen and oxygen over a wide range of pH [29]. It is also necessary that during this reaction there will be no such side products which deteriorate the environment.

In this study, we reveal the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified CuCo alloy nanoparticles for water splitting applications. As carbon dots have large scale availability, facile synthesis, good surface to volume ratio, non-toxic and are cost effective. These features makes them promising candidates as electrocatalysts. Moreover, characteristic electron transfer capability and variety of functional groups (hydroxyl, amine and carboxyl group) on surface offer many feasible sites to fabricate into a multicomponent electrocatalysts [30]. The as synthesized ecofriendly catalyst possesses inexpensive precursors and exhibit highly efficient electrocatalytic activity in alkaline media. It is worth mentioning here that this novel combination possesses over potentials as low as 0.38 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in pH 13 medium which is best among the non-metal OER electrocatalysts. Detailed electrochemical and physical studies indicate that the high OER activity of the nitrogen/carbon materials is from the pyridinic-N- or/and Quaternary-N-related active sites. The concept of the non-metal electrocatalyst is further identified using nitrogen-contained graphene materials for OER process.

Ethanol amine (C2H7NO) 98%, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) 30%, copper II acetate monohydrate (C4H6CuO4.H2O) 99%, cobalt II acetate, potassium hydroxide (KOH) 99%, nafion solution (C7HF13O5S) 5%, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) 99%, potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) 99% were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) 98% was obtained from BHD Chemical. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) 99.0% was purchased from Merck. Milli-Q water was obtained from Millipore System. All chemicals were used as received.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano SEM 450) was used to investigate the structure and morphology of samples at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV and 200 kV, respectively. FTIR analysis is performed via a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 4.5 mL of hydrogen peroxide and 3 mL of ethanolamine (source of carbon and nitrogen) was taken in a beaker and placed on heating mantle at 260 °C for 20 min to get NDCDs. At initial stage the color of ethanolamine was light yellow but when H2O2 (as oxidant) was added in it then the color change and turn into dark brown. During this process H2O2 was completely consumed and water was evaporated. Then dark brown colloidal solid was obtained which was diluted with 200 mL distilled water. The doped carbon material was centrifuged, washed with water and ethanol, dried in air and stored for further use.

CuCo Alloy Nanoparticles were synthesized by following hydrothermal synthesis. Simply, 3.75 g of Cu (II) acetate hydrate Cu(OCCH).HO and 10.895 g of Cobalt (II) acetate tetrahydrate Co(OCCH).4H2O) were mixed in 250 mL ethylene glycol. The mixture was placed in autoclave at 180–190 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, CuCo Alloy nanoparticles were washed with deionized water and alcohol, repeatedly and dried in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon dots (NDCDs)-CuCo alloy nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: N-doped carbon dots (NDCDs)-CuCo alloy nanoparticles were synthesized by pyrolizing the materials at various temperatures of 300 °C, 400 °C, and 500 °C. Nitrogen doped carbon dots and CuCo alloy (12 mg) was mixed in 1:12 ratio and the homogenous solution was put in a crucible and kept at 300 °C in a furnace for 3 h. NDCDs-CuCo alloy material was collected and used for further analysis. Samples were also prepared following same procedure at 400 °C and 500 °C at optimized ratio of 1:12 of nitrogen doped carbon dots and CuCo respectively, for 3 h.

Electrochemical investigations of electrocatalytic material synthesized at various temperatures (300 °C, 400 °C, and 500 °C) were performed on Metroham Autolab with three electrode systems consisting of working, counter and reference electrodes. The glassy carbon electrode with the geometrical diameter of 3 mm was used as working electrode. For the characterization of these samples the parameters which were optimized includes potential window ranges (from −1 V to +1 V) and scan rate (varies from 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s). The electrocatalytic activity was observed in alkaline media of 1 M KOH and 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution. Generally, for modification of working electrode surface following procedures was used to prepare slurry, 4 mg of catalyst and 80 μL of Nafion as a binder were sonicated for 30 min to form dispersion. The mixture was loaded onto electrode surface, and was dried. OER and HER activity of as prepared electrocatalyst was measured in 1.0 M KOH alkaline solution, respectively. Linear sweep voltammetry was used to investigate electrocatalytic activity of nitrogen doped carbon dot modified CuCo alloy particles at scan rate of 5mVs−1. All electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature 26 ± 1 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphological characterization of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The ratio of NDCD with CuCo alloy nanoparticle was selected 1:12 and synthesized by following chemical and pyrolysis methods. It has been reported [31,32] earlier that the synthesis temperature not only affects the composition of nitrogen doped carbon dots modified CuCo alloy nanoparticles, but greatly influences the morphology. Therefore, material was synthesized at different temperature of 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C. After preparing powder samples for NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles external morphology and electrocatalytic activity of the materials was investigated. External morphology of nitrogen doped carbon dots and its composites with bimetallic alloy was investigated with SEM. Fig. 1 (a, b) shows the external morphology and size of NDCDs and CuCo alloy nanoparticles. NDCDs show heterogeneous morphology. Fig. 1(c, d, e) shows the comparison of external morphologies of NDCDs composites with metal alloy of CuCo synthesized at three different temperatures (300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C). This comparison gave the information about the effect of temperature on NDCDs-CuCo alloy morphology. As temperature increased from 300 °C to 500 °C, the compact and smooth CuCo alloy became heterogeneous and uneven. This heterogeneous and uneven external surface decreases the electrochemical water splitting. The SEM results shows that NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles synthesized at lower temperature can be a promising material for electrochemical water splitting.

FTIR analyses of NDCDs are also carried out (Fig. S1a), the spectra show peak at 1668 cm−1 which confirms that the CO stretching of amide bond and at 1570 cm−1(N−H in-plane bending) vibrational fingerprints centered which confirms the presence of amide groups. The peak at 1418 cm−1 shows the bending absorption peak of OH group, the peak at 1635.74 cm−1 and 1733.65 cm−1 indicate symmetrical medium extending vibration absorption peak of CN and CO band respectively. Fig. S1(b) peak at 1703.0 cm−1 due to carboxylic group indicate the interaction between the metal ions and carbonyl group. Cu (II) and Co(II) show bands at 1560.0 cm−1 and 1560.3 cm−1 respectively, due to the coordination of carboxylate groups with copper and cobalt ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical activity of NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles for water splitting
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity of as prepared materials was investigated by modifying bare carbon electrode surface. The electrode surface was modified with thin film consist of catalyst/nafion ink for electrochemical characterization. The catalyst loading amount is only 0.8 mg cm−2. The low catalyst loading indicating that the NDCDs-CuCo nanoparticles is a promising electrocatalyst. The electrochemical response is monitored by cyclic voltammetry and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV). Parameters like scan rate, loading and temperature dependent type of material are optimized. The electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs, CuCo alloy nanoparticles and NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at different temperatures (300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C) observed at different pH of 7.4, 7.8 and 8. (Fig. 2 ).

NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C show significant higher redox potential at pH -8 as compared to other samples as shown in Fig. S2(c) against silver chloride as reference electrode. However, electrocatalytic activity of material is decreases at lower pH value of 7.4 and 7.8 (Fig. S2a, b). The graph between RHE and current density of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at optimized conditions is shown in Fig. S2(a′), (b′) and (c′). Best electrocatalytic activity in both cases was obtained at pH −8 which is selected for further experiments.

The voltammetric response and linear calibration curve of different concentration of NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C is determined and voltammogram are shown in Fig. 3 (a, b). The reversible cyclic voltammetric peaks obtained at different concentrations of NDCDs-CuCo alloy modified at 300 °C. The oxidation peak current for blank Ipa = 0.000705108 mA. The oxidation peak current observed for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles modified at 300 °C is increased from Ipa = 0.002885 mA to Ipa = 0.005969 mA for concentration from 0.1 μL to 0.5 μL respectively. The peak potential for blank and NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C is Epa = 0.60972 V and Epa = 0.139 V respectively. For NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles modified at 300 °C the value of reduction peak current Ipc = −0.004074 mA, Ipc = −0.004495 mA, Ipc = −0.005684 mA, Ipc = −0.007504 mA and Ipc = −0.008705 mA for concentration from 0.1 μL to 0.5 μL respectively. The peak potential for blank is Epc = −0.5462 V. For NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles modified at 300 °C the cathodic peaks potential Epc = −0.018 V is observed. It indicates that as the amount of NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C increases the rate of oxidation and reduction also increases due to the availability of more number of oxidizing and reducing species at the GCE. The graph between concentration and current at pH of 8 shows the linear relationship. The calibration curve also predicts the linear relationship between current and concentration in (0.1 μL to 0.5 μL) with correlation coefficient r2 = 0.88165. The slop value 1.0718E−5 with standard error of 1.73305E−6 and the intercept for the graph is 1.35915E-6 with standard error of 5.24705E−7 observed for oxidation. In case of reduction, the correlation coefficient r2 = 0.94366 is observed in concentration range from 0.1 μL to 0.5 μL. The slop value −1.45302E−5 with standard error of 1.5783E−6 and the intercept value −1.58277E−6 with standard error of 4.77855E−7 is observed.

NDCDs modified electrode was dipped into 1 M KOH solution and electrochemical response was obtained at different scan rate from 0.06 to 0.1 mV/s. In Fig. S3(a) the resultant voltammogram show prominent oxidation and reduction peak response of NDCDs. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.06 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0448916 mA at potential of Epa = 0.608993 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.0334365 mA at potential of Epc = −1.101661 V. Change in potential ∆E = Epa – Epc = 0.608993- (−1.101661) = −1.710654 V. The scan rate 0.06 mV/s is slow and consume much time for the electrochemical peak response. Due to this increase in time interval, surface of GCE loaded with sample is affected by deposition and results of impurities may be not appropriate. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.07 mV/s oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0517027 mA at potential of Epa = 0.588465 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.0402476 mA at potential of Epc = −1.252199 V with ∆E = 1.840664 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.08 mV/s show prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.959752 mA at potential of Epa = 0.766373 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.538699 mA at potential of Epc = −1.35826 V with ∆E = 1.305072 V. Furthermore, the voltammogram obtained at 0.09 mV/s scan rate show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.216873 mA at potential of Epa = 0.945992 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.0606811 mA at potential of Epc = −1.358260 V. The voltammogram obtained at fastest scan rate of 0.1 mV/s show electrochemical response in less time interval with oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.262693 mA at potential of Epa = 0.8450639 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.0995356 mA at potential of Epc = −1.365107 V with ∆E = 2.2101709 V. The graph of NDCDs between current and scan rate at optimized conditions show the linear relationship as shown in Fig. S3(b). The correlation coefficient is r2 = 0.88228 and the slop value obtained is 0.00601 with standard error of 0.00108. The intercept value is −3.46191E−4 with standard error of 8.76884E−5 in oxidation, while in case of reduction the correlation coefficient is r2 = 0.83136. The slop value obtained is −0.00153 with standard error of 3.35325E−4. The value of intercept is −6.45512E−5 with standard error of 2.72419E−5. The graph of NDCDs between RHE and current density at optimized conditions shown in Fig. S3(c). As scan rate increases from 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s the catalytic activity increases.

Electrochemical experiment involved measurement of electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs in alkaline media of (1 M KOH) as shown in Fig. S4(a). NDCDs at 0.1 mV/s exhibit onset potential of 1.812897 V toward OER, with anodic current that rises rapidly as more potential is applied. The result reveals that the onset potential of NDCDs is very close to the thermodynamic potential of OER (i.e., 1.23 V). Accordingly, NDCDs give a Tafel slope (334 mV/decade) and a current density (9.56 ∗ 10−1 mA/cm2) as shown in Fig. S4(b). Additionally, NDCDs afford current densities of 1 mA/cm2 at over potential (η) = 0.58778 V. The reversible potential from HER and OER are 0 and 1.23 V vs. reference hydrogen electrode (RHE) respectively. Hence, the electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs increases from scan rate 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s as current density increases.

Electrochemical response of CuCo alloy in 1 M KOH is shown in Fig. S5(a). It is observed that at lower scan rate of 0.06 mV/s minor oxidation or reduction occurs. As the scan rate increase from 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s proper oxidation and reduction peaks observed. The resultant voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.06 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.620868 mA at potential of Epa = −0.1191 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.473914 mA at potential of Epc = −0.9586 V. Change in potential ∆E = Epa − Epc = −0.1191-(−0.473914 = 0.593014 V. The scan rate 0.06 mV/s is slow and consume much time for providing the electrochemical peak response. Due to this increase in time interval surface of GCE loaded with sample is affected by deposition and results of impurities may be not appropriate. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.07 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 1.62 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0081 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.790904 mA at potential of Epc = −0.8175 V with ∆E = 0.782804 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.08 mV/s showing oxidation peak current Ipa = 2.15 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0081 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.979241 mA at potential of Epc = −0.8175 V with ∆E = 0.8094 V. Furthermore, the voltammogram obtained at 0.09 mV/s scan rate show prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 2.59 mA at potential of Epa = 0.0081 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −1.03 mA at potential of Epc = −0.8248 V with ∆E = 0.8329 V. The voltammogram obtained at fastest scan rate of 0.1 mV/s show electrochemical response in less time interval with oxidation peak current Ipa = 3.15 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0874 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −2.13 mA at potential of Epc = −0.9666 V with ∆E = 0.8792 V. The graph of CuCo alloy nanoparticles between current and scan rate at optimized conditions show the linear relationship as shown in Fig. S5 (b) with correlation coefficient was r2 = 0.96844, slop of 0.06255 with standard error of 0.00562. The value of intercept is −0.00296 with standard error of 4.56788E−4 is observed in oxidation, while in case of reduction the correlation coefficient is r2 = 0.8742 and slop of −0.03699. The graph of CuCo alloy nanoparticles between RHE and current density at optimized conditions shown in Fig. S5(c). As scan rate increases from 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s the catalytic activity increases.

Electrochemical activity of CuCo alloy nanoparticles in alkaline media of (1 M KOH) is shown in Fig. S6(a). CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 0.1 mV/s exhibit onset potential of 1.64444 V toward OER, with anodic current that rises rapidly as more potential is applied. The result reveals that the onset potential of CuCo alloy nanoparticles is very close to the thermodynamic potential of OER (i.e., 1.23 V). Accordingly, CuCo alloy nanoparticles give a Tafel slope (265 mV/decade) and a current density (3.89 mA/cm2) as shown in Fig. S6(b). Additionally, CuCo alloy nanoparticles afford current densities of 1.07 mA/cm2 at over potential ((η) = 0.370495 V. Hence, the electrocatalytic activity of CuCo alloy nanoparticles increases from scan rate 0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s as current density increases.

Electrochemical response of NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles modified at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C suspension is also observed in 1 M KOH solution at different scan rate from 0.06 to 0.1 mV/s. In Fig. 4(a) the resultant voltammogram for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C obtained at scan rate of 0.06 mV/s showing prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0505893 mA at potential of Epa = −0.9358 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.00833788 mA at potential of Epc = −0.3821 V with ∆E = −0.5535 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.07 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.236689 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0221 V and reduction peaks current Ipc = −0.1298825 mA at potential of Epc = −0.9506 V with ∆E = 0.9285 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.08 mV/s showing prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.3357689 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0695 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.12913 mA at potential of Epc = −0.9708 V with ∆E = 0.9013 V.

Furthermore, the voltammogram at 0.09 mV/s scan rate show increased electrochemical response as compare to 0.08 mV/s scan rate. At 0.09 mV/s scan rate prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.33576 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0695 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.400234 mA at potential of Epc = −0.9607 V with ∆E = 0.8912 V. The voltammogram obtained at fastest scan rate of 0.1 mV/s show electrochemical response in less time interval with oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.395768 mA at potential of Epa = −0.0828 V and reduction peak current Ipc = −0.424952 mA at potential of Epc = −1.0618 V with ∆E = 0.979 V. for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C as shown in Fig. 4(a). The graph of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles prepared at 300 °C between current and scan rate at optimized conditions show the linear relationship r2 = 0.96502 as shown in Fig. 4(a′). It reveals that on increasing the scan rate the resultant electrochemical response increases. Correlation coefficient for oxidation and reduction are 0.96502 and 0.7924 respectively.

Similarly the voltammetric peak response and linear calibration curve for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 400 °C and 500 °C observed at different scan rates (0.06 mV/s to 0.1 mV/s) in 1 M KOH solution as shown in Fig. 4 (b, b′) and (c, c′) respectively. In Fig. 4(b) the resultant voltammogram for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 400 °C obtained at scan rate of 0.06 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0361922 mA at potential of Epa = 0.5867 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.07 mV/s showing prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.06084403 mA at potential of Epa = −0.1123 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.08 mV/s show prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0687653 mA at potential of Epa = −0.1224 V. Furthermore, the voltammogram of 0.09 mV/s scan rate obtained prominent oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0738186 mA at potential of Epa = −0.18234 V. The graph of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 400 °C between current and scan rate at optimized conditions show the linear relationship as shown in Fig. 4(b′) with correlation coefficient was r2 = 0.89495.

In Fig. 4(c) the resultant voltammogram for NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 500 °C obtained at scan rate of 0.06 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0246517 mA at potential of Epa = −0.1722 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.07 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.027724 mA at potential of Epa = −0.22206 V. The voltammogram obtained at scan rate of 0.08 mV/s show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.065419 mA at potential of Epa = −0.1722 V.

Furthermore, the voltammogram at 0.09 mV/s scan rate show oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.0119707 mA at potential of Epa = −0.17223 V. The voltammogram obtained at fastest scan rate of 0.1 mV/s show electrochemical response in less time interval with oxidation peak current Ipa = 0.1264132 mA at potential of Epa = 0.16213 V. The graph of NDCD-CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 500 °C between current and scan rate at optimized conditions show the linear relationship as shown in Fig. 4 (c′) with correlation coefficient is r2 = 0.91567. As the scan rate increase the electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C also increase. Hence 0.1 mV/s considered as optimized scan rate for better electrochemical response.

Cyclic voltammetric curves plotted against potential (V vs RHE) and current density involved measurement of electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs-CuCo at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C in alkaline media of 1 M KOH (pH = 13.65) as shown in Fig. S7 (supporting information). The graph of NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C between potential (V vs RHE) and current density at optimized conditions shown in Fig. S7(a), (b) and (c).

Electrochemical experiment by LSV involved measurement of electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C in alkaline media of (1 M KOH) as shown in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5(a), NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles (300 °C) at 0.1 mV/s exhibit onset potential of 1.671295 V toward OER, anodic current that rises rapidly as more potential is applied. Accordingly, CuCo alloy give Tafel slope (277 mV/decade) and current density (0.6189591 mA/cm2) as shown in Fig. 5(a′). Both the Tafel slope and current density of an electrocatalyst for a specific electrocatalytic study of interest provide many insights on the kinetic behavior of the electrochemical process. The Tafel slope for example, is usually used to semi-quantitatively predict how much faster the reaction occurs on an electrocatalytic interface. Additionally, CuCo afford current density of 1 mA/cm2 at over potential (η) = 0.377819 V). These findings demonstrate good electrocatalytic activity of CuCo toward OER. Hence, the electrocatalytic activity of CuCo alloy nanoparticles increases from scan rate 0.06 to 0.1 mV/s as current density increases. As shown in Fig. 5(b), NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles (400 °C) at 0.1 mV/s exhibit onset potential of 1.639557 V toward OER, anodic current that rises rapidly as more potential is applied. Accordingly, CuCo alloy give Tafel slope (262 mV/decade) and current density (0.723313 mA/cm2) as shown in Fig. 5(b′).

CuCo afford current density of 1 mA/cm2 at over potential (η) = 0.358288 V). These findings demonstrate good electrocatalytic activity of CuCo toward OER. Similarly, NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles (500 °C) at 0.1 mV/s exhibit onset potential of 1.829987 V toward OER, anodic current that rises rapidly as more potential is applied. Accordingly, CuCo alloy give Tafel slope (341 mV/decade) and current density (−1.16107 mA/cm2) as shown in Fig. 5(c′). Hence, the electrocatalytic activity of CuCo alloy nanoparticles increases from scan rate 0.06 to 0.1 mV/s as current density increases Fig. 5(c).

The peak response for NDCDs, CuCo alloy nanoparticles and NDCDs modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles at 300 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C are shown in Fig. 6 . Generally, as the pyrolysis temperature increase from 300 °C to 500 °C, the material show decreasing order of catalytic activity, because the over potential slowly rises up which was necessary to produce current density. Apart from this, it also assumed that nitrogen doping in carbon materials is highly affected by temperature. The increase in pyrolysis temperature decrease nitrogen content in nitrogen doped carbon dots and hence decreases electroanalytical properties of material [25–26]. When materials are pyrolyzed above 300 °C, it is evident from the figure that electrocatalytic activity of NDCDs modified -CuCo alloy nanoparticles affected at optimized conditions.

In this study, we have reported the non-noble metal based electrocatalyst for both HER and OER. The Nitrogen decorated Carbon Dots (NDCDs) modified with CuCo alloy nanoparticles by facile approach that proved highly effective, non-noble metal-based electrocatalyst for water splitting are fabricated. The NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles possess elements which are easily available and synthesized by economical and available method. NDCDs-CuCo alloy electrocatalyst showed redox activity under optimized conditions of concentration, scan rate, temperature and pH. The optimized value of pH was pH = 8 which proved the ability of as-synthesized electrocatalyst to work in basic media. NDCDs-CuCo alloy nanoparticles offered good redox peak value, for HER and OER, at pH 8 and 0.1 mV/s. The modification was supported and characterized by using SEM and FTIR spectroscopy. Conclusively, the electrocatalyst is cost effective, non-noble metal based, facile to synthesize and electroactive for water splitting, offers low over potential, in basic media.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Sayed Tayyab Raza Naqvi:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Tahir Rasheed:Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.Saadat Majeed:Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.Zubia Rani:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Rahat Nawaz:Data curation, Writing - original draft.Muhammad Naeem Ashiq:Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors describe no conflict of interest in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was financially supported by Office of Research Innovation and Commercialization (ORIC), Bahauddin Zakariya University Multan, Pakistan under grant No. DR & EL/D-740 and Institute of Chemical Sciences, Bahauddin Zakariya University Multan, Pakistan.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.113111.
